data.title: Celsius Holdings Bottom Is Here - Growth Setup Looks Promising
data.description:
CELH's Reversal May Already Be Here Thanks To The Alani Acquisition, As The Bulls Also Defend A Floor

We previously covered Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) in April 2025, discussing how the stock's trading floor had seemingly materialized, thanks to the robust performance metrics reported in FY2024 and the accretive Alani Nu acquisition.

Combined with the rich Free Cash Flow generation, the promising consensus forward estimates, and the reasonable valuations, the stock may deliver more than doubling upside potential ahead.

CELH 1Y Stock Price

Since then, CELH has failed to break out of its next resistance levels of $37s ranges as expected, albeit seemingly well-supported at its 50/ 200 day moving averages.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the recent closing of the Alani Nu acquisition on April 01, 2025 and the bottoming of its performance metrics after the painful PepsiCo (PEP) inventory adjustments since May 2024.

For reference, CELH reported overall FQ1'25 revenues of $329.3M (-0.8% QoQ/ -7.4% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 21.1% (+2.2 points QoQ/ -3.6 YoY), and adj EPS of $0.18 (+28.5% QoQ/ -33.3% YoY).

These numbers mark its second consecutive quarter of improved top/ bottom-line performances after the trough FQ3'24 numbers of $265.7M (-33.9% QoQ/ -30.9% YoY), 1.6% (-23.3 points QoQ/ -25.3 YoY), and $0.00 (compared to a quarter ago at $0.28 / a year ago at $0.30), respectively.

While CELH continues to report a sustained impact of PEP's inventory correction in the North American region's revenues of $306.5M in FQ1'25 (-1.7% QoQ/ -9.7% YoY), it is apparent that the headwind may be occurring across the board, as the sugar-free energy drink competition intensifies and the promotional activities increase.

The same has been observed in Monster Beverage's (MNST) decelerating sales growth in the UCAN region at $4.32B in FY2024 (+2.8% YoY), compared to FY2023 levels at $4.2B (+10.5% YoY).

Otherwise, CELH continues to report higher US energy drink category dollar share of 16.2% on a pro forma basis in Q1'25 (+1.9 points YoY), based on Celsius at 10.9% (-0.5 points YoY) and Alani Nu at 5.3% (+2.4 points YoY), compared to MNST at 27.6% (-0.7 points YoY) and Red Bull at 37.1% (+0.4 points YoY).

These reasons are also why we believe that the Alani Nu acquisition is likely to be highly strategic while accretive to its overall energy drink portfolio's performance/ financial metrics from Q2'25 onwards.

At the same time, CELH has been able to successfully scale its international presence as well, as observed in the growing international revenues of $22.8M (+12.3% QoQ/ +40.7% YoY) across the legacy EMEA markets and the newly launched markets, including the UK, Ireland, France, Australia, and New Zealand.

Combined with the improving bottom-lines, new flavors, ongoing promotional events for multi-pack offerings, growing retail shelf space by between +15% to +20%, and new secondary placements near checkout lines, we can understand why the market is cautiously optimistic about its future recovery, as observed in the stock holding on to much of its recent March/ April 2025 gains.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, it is unsurprising why the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with CELH expected to chart an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +28.6%/ +26.4% through FY2027, compared to the prior estimates of +14.7%/ +22.2%, respectively, likely attributed to the accretive Alani Nu acquisition from FQ2'25 onwards.

On the other hand, it is undeniable that CELH's growth profile is already maturing compared to its 5Y historical growth at +79.5%/ +103.6%, respectively.

This is worsened by the potential tariff impact on the aluminum cans and its COGS/ gross margins from H2'25 onwards, despite the regionalized manufacturing capabilities and the prior acquisition of Big Beverages' manufacturing facility in the US.

Given that CELH's inventory correction is likely to continue through Q2'25 before easing in H2'25, the company may potentially underperform in FY2025 as well, against its pro forma FY2024 revenues of $1.95B and adj EBITDA of $392.7M along with our prior FY2027 adj EBITDA estimates of $880M (3Y CAGR of +31%).

On the other hand, the tariff war has shown promising signs of de-escalation with the UK and China, amongst others, with it signaling that the worst of the recessionary/ stagflation fears may already be behind us.

CELH Valuations

For now, the decelerating growth trends may also be why the CELH stock has been discounted to FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 21.85x and our estimated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x, based on the $37.32 stock prices at the time of writing and the consensus FY2025 adj EPS estimates of $0.90 (+28.5% YoY).

This is compared to its 1Y mean of 26.13x/ 40.55x and the 5Y mean of 106.62x/ 316.60x, albeit up from the recent bottom of 16.92x/ 25.33x, respectively.

On the one hand, our estimated CELH's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x may seem to be somewhat expensive, when compared to the sector median of 16.18x and the energy drinks/ beverage peers, including PEP at 16.37x, Coca-Cola Company (KO) at 23.78x, and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) at 16.48x, aside from MNST at 32.80x.

On the other hand, based on our estimated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x and the consensus adj EPS growth at a CAGR of +26.4%, CELH appears to be cheap at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.57x, compared to its 5Y mean of 3.05x.

When compared to the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.63x and the energy drinks/ beverage peers, including MNST at 2.69x, PEP at 4.78x, KO at 3.93x, and KDP at 2.29x, it is apparent that CELH remains a compelling high-growth investment thesis here.

So, Is CELH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CELH 5Y Stock Price

For now, CELH has already lost -60.4% or the equivalent -$12.78B of its market capitalization from the 2024 heights, albeit seemingly finding a floor at the February 2025 bottom of $21 ranges.

For context, we had previously offered a bull-case long-term price target of $79.30 in our prior article, based on the projected expansion in its adj EBITDA per share to $3.70 by FY2027 (expanding at a CAGR of +30.8%) and the prior FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 21.44x.

With it remaining to be seen when CELH's inventory correction may last, worsened by the intermediate term uncertainty surrounding the intensified tariff war, it appears that our prior estimates may have been overly aggressive, as the consensus also projects a much slower 3Y adj EBITDA growth at a pro forma CAGR of +17.8% ahead.

Despite so, based on our estimated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 41.46x and the consensus' current FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $1.41 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +26.4%), we are still looking at a relatively promising upside potential of +56.4% to our long-term price target of $58.40.

These estimates do not seem to be overly aggressive as well, given Alani Nu's FY2024 top-line growth by +62.7% YoY, with it likely to jump start CELH's stagnant growth profile.

Combined with the materialization of bullish support at the prior $21 ranges and the moderating short interest volumes by -30.3% from recent heights, we are cautiously reiterating our Buy rating for the CELH stock.

data.title: Realty Income: Shareholders Deserve To Be Rewarded With More Upside
data.description:
With a market capitalization of $50.95 billion, Realty Income (NYSE:O) is a giant in the REIT space. Over the years, the company has achieved remarkable growth, with acquisitions of other major players in the industry helping to make that possible. Recently, the quality of the business has been on display. You see, back in February of this year, I decided to revisit the firm. This came after shares had dipped slightly despite will sting continued growth in revenue. At the time, I argued that the small drop the stock experienced, combined with the quality of the business and how shares were priced, justified a bullish outlook. Ultimately, I ended up reaffirming my ‘buy’ rating on it.

Since the time that article was published, Realty Income has outperformed the broader market. Shares are up 3.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 5.4%. This comes on the back of growth in revenue, profits, and cash flows. The current trajectory that the company is on looks promising and, with net leverage in the same range as other players, it is certainly deserving of attention. Admittedly, shares are not as cheap as some of the other players in the industry and those firms might make for better prospects. But just because one company might be better does not mean that another doesn't justify a bullish outlook. Because of these factors, I believe that maintaining the company as a soft ‘buy’ makes sense at this time.

Taking another look at Realty Income

Since my last article about Realty Income, investors have been provided with data covering one additional fiscal quarter. That happens to be the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, the company saw revenue come in at $1.38 billion. That's an increase of 9.5% compared to the $1.26 billion reported the previous year. This was driven largely by a $90.2 million rise in rental revenue that the company generated.

On a same store basis, revenue grew from $1.14 billion to $1.15 billion. This increase of $14.3 million came from lease renewals and rent escalations that the business received from tenants. An even bigger driver of these sales increased during this time came from new properties acquired between 2024 and 2025. All told, the company purchased 497 properties that added a combined $52.4 million of additional revenue on a year-over-year basis. Of course, in order to fund these purchases, management also divested of certain assets.

This included 361 properties that were sold during the relevant window of time. This negatively impacted revenue to the tune of $12.4 million. After factoring in these sales, Realty Income saw the number of properties it has grown from 15,485 to 15,627. It is worth noting that, year over year, the enterprise saw its occupancy rate remain almost flat, dipping ever so slightly from 98.6% to 98.5%. In the chart below, you can see how consistent occupancy has been over the last couple of years. This serves as a testament to the high-quality nature of the assets that the company has and its track record of selling off assets that no longer make sense for its portfolio.

The growth in revenue also brought with it a rise in profitability. I maintain that net income is not all that relevant when it comes to REITs. But for illustrative purposes, from the first quarter of 2024 to the first quarter of 2025, net profits for the company improved from $129.7 million to $249.8 million. Obviously, the rise in revenue contributed here. But another contributor was the fact that, in the first quarter of 2024, the company incurred $94.1 million in merger, transaction, and other related costs, that were related to its acquisition of Spirit Realty Capital in a deal valued at $6.19 billion. This excluded the net debt that Spirit Realty Capital had on its books.

Other profitability metrics rose as well. Operating cash flow ticked up modestly from $778.7 million to $787.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $785.4 million to $941.4 million. There are other profitability metrics that we should take into consideration as well. As an example, there is FFO, or funds from operations. This metric expanded from $785.7 million to $937.7 million. On an adjusted basis, it grew from $862.9 million to $949.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business went from $1.15 billion to $1.26 billion.

This is a fantastic showing for the company and serves as a testament to its quality and growth prospects. For the rest of this year, management has provided some guidance, but not much. On a per share basis, adjusted FFO should be around $4.22 to $4.28. At the midpoint, this would imply reading of $3.80 billion. For context, last year, this figure came in at $3.62 billion. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise at the same rate, we would expect FFO to be around $3.64 billion, adjusted operating cash flow to be $3.72 billion, and EBITDA to total $5.06 billion.

The year-over-year growth can certainly be attributed in part to the aforementioned acquisition of Spirit Realty Capital. However, management continues to invest in growing its asset base. In fact, this year, the company is planning to allocate around $4 billion toward acquisition activities. For the first quarter of this year, the company has already made purchases of $1.18 billion. This excludes $22.9 million in improvements allocated toward real estate that it already owns and another $5.3 million that it has invested in unconsolidated entities. Investments in loans also came in at $200.9 million. This doesn't mean that the company is only interested in buying. As I mentioned earlier, it's not afraid to sell assets. But during the quarter, it only divested of $92.6 million worth of properties.

If we use the estimates that we have calculated for 2025, and we also use historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of Realty Income are currently priced. Typically, these are multiples that I would expect a ‘hold’ candidate to have, not a ‘buy’. However, I do allow some wiggle room for high-quality companies, especially if they are continuing to grow. Relative to similar companies, Realty Income is perhaps slightly on the high side of the spectrum. In the table below, I compared it to five such firms. On a price to operating cash flow basis, I found that four of the five companies ended up being cheaper than Realty Income is. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, this number totaled three of the five.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Realty Income 14.4 16.2
Simon Property Group (SPG) 14.0 15.3
Kimco Realty (KIM) 13.3 16.5
Regency Centers (REG) 16.8 18.2
Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) 12.9 15.5
NNN REIT (NNN) 11.9 14.7

I would like to point out, however, that the range of multiples for these companies really is not that vast. This is especially true when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach. As an example, if Realty Income were to trade at the lowest multiple of the five competitors, then the EV to EBITDA approach would imply downside of 13.8%. The downside using the price to operating cash flow approach would be a bit more at 17.3%. By comparison, using the highest multiple of the five companies, upside would be 16.7% for the price to operating cash flow scenario, and it would be 19.3% when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach. This places the company pretty solidly in the middle of the range compared to these other firms.

Another thing to keep in mind is that the net leverage ratio of the business is within range of the competition as well. In the chart below, you can see that its net leverage ratio is 5.54. Only two of the five competitors are lower than our candidate is at this time. But the overall range in difference between these six firms is remarkably small, indicating that at least they are all of a similar level of safety.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I believe that Reality Income is a solid business that will continue to grow as time goes on. It might not be the most attractive opportunity, even in its own space. But between the quality that the company has exhibited and its continued growth, I believe that its current pricing justifies a soft ‘buy’ rating right now.

data.title: If CoreWeave Wasn't Upping CapEx, We'd Have A Problem: Double Down
data.description:
CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV), the new kid on the block backed by big brother Nvidia, reported its first quarter results after the bell Wednesday, and investors were not happy. The stock crashed as much as 9% in pre-market trading, making for a window to add on the sell-off.

This quarter, CoreWeave revenue grew faster than expected, up 420% from a year ago to $981.6M versus $853M expected. More importantly, management gave us what we were waiting for, which is a strong pipeline: OpenAI committed to a five-year deal worth up to $11.9B with CoreWeave and a $4B contract for the first quarter. We upgraded CoreWeave from sell to buy back in early April (after shorting it into the IPO), and since the stock is up 54% versus the S&P 500, which is up 10%. Our belief was that this fresh company wouldn’t have gone for an IPO if a solid pipeline to expand growth wasn’t already in the works, and yesterday proved that to be true. CoreWeave’s revenue backlog is up 63% for Q1 to $25.9B.

What we didn’t see coming, however, was investor panic over high spending. We did flag the problem of upfront CapEx spend when it comes to the neocloud guys (this includes Nebius (NBIS)), which comes from their need to keep spending on the newest generation AI GPUs to be able to charge higher rental prices and make a profit. So even though this is the logical necessity of the neocloud peer group, the market was still caught off guard when CoreWeave called for CapeEx of $20B-$23B for the year, which is much higher than the $18.3B projected by Wall Street. This is what management said to ease the higher spend concerns:

That is a real articulation of new uptake, new clients coming on board to buy more infrastructure from us over the balance of the year…

Our take on the higher spend is that it is necessary if CoreWeave wants to compete in any meaningful way with the bigger players like Amazon (AMZN) AWS. Because the depreciation of the GPUs will happen very quickly, we’ve pointed this out when discussing the crash of H100/H200 renting prices when we wrote on Nebius. So while CoreWeave sits on something like 250,000 Hopper GPUs, they need to make this GPU farm have more Blackwell. The real alarm wasn’t just the spend, it was also how fast it would jump between Q1 versus Q2. This quarter, management spent CapEx of $1.9B, and in Q2, management is expecting to spend $3B to $3.5B.

Management is guiding CoreWeave to make $4.9B to $5.1B for FY25, which would mean a 363% growth rate and which falls comfortably ahead of Wall Street’s expectations for $4.61B. For next quarter, management is also guiding above consensus for $1.06B to $1.1B in revenue higher than the anticipated $1.04B. We like these numbers but we're still tracking our main concerns.

One of our top concerns for CoreWeave that we mentioned in our first sell call on CoreWeave post-IPO was high customer concentration, with Microsoft (MSFT) making up 62% of revenue for 2024. Management pushed out this concern for us on the call, saying:

At the end of Q1, there was no entity that was more than 50% of our backlog. That with the new contract that's coming through, we do not think that that will be the case on a go forward basis.

What we know is that CoreWeave’s big clients are obviously Microsoft, as well as IBM (IBM) and Nvidia (NVDA). Analysts from D.A. Davidson pointed out:

While the revenue from Microsoft is likely secure for the next three years, CoreWeave represents overflow capacity for Microsoft, which may not need that capacity in the future.

And while this is true, it won’t show up in the near-term in our opinion, especially not after the market just priced in the negatives of higher spend, shown below with CoreWeave's pre-market movement, recovered a bit from the -9% it was earlier. This is a risk for CoreWeave alongside the tier 1 competition from AWS and other players that can afford upfront CapEx that CoreWeave cannot sustainably guarantee. However, we don’t think it’s a negative that’ll weigh down CoreWeave this year.

What’s not to like?

Easy: High cash burn & High valuation

High debt was our second concern for CoreWeave, and we don’t think there’s any easing of that concern. For the quarter, CoreWeave accumulated $12B in debt commitments with an insane interest rate of 10%-14%. The way CoreWeave does this is by taking debt against Nvidia’s GPUs as collateral to give Nvidia more money, i.e., buying more GPUs. This comes hand in hand with the bigger issue of making money, which we need to see more of and which a healthy pipeline should support in the near-term. The company is still losing money now; this quarter's adjusted net loss was $150M, more than triple the expected $41.7M loss, shown below.

Valuation is also pricey, but that’s normal for anything with an AI sticker on it, let alone backed by Nvidia itself. Forward EV/Sales is 8.79x versus the group average of 2.89x.

What to do with the sell-off?

The simple answer is buy it, but don’t hold it forever. This is a stock to trade because, longer term, there is a higher risk profile for CoreWeave from the consistent need for upfront CapEx to counter tech being outdated, high competition from bigger players, and high cash burn. Yes, all that sounds bad, but we don’t think it’ll weigh on the stock in the near-term, and the panic on CapEx spend has already been priced into the stock and overshadowed the actual reported growth.

data.title: CarGurus: People Aren't Talking About Competition Enough
data.description:
The Set Up

CarGurus (NASDAQ:CARG) has positioned itself as a leading online automotive marketplace, connecting buyers and sellers of both new and used vehicles. Upon an initial look, the company appears to have a SaaS-like business model which includes a marketplace platform that emphasizes transparency and data-driven insights to help customers understand the price vs. value tradeoff when purchasing a new vehicle. They also take a consumer-first approach where they provide search results based on relevance and deal quality rather than showing paid listings. However, I believe that the company's growth prospects are currently being overestimated and there are both long and short term headwinds on the horizon.

CarGurus greatly benefitted from the post-pandemic era where a global chip shortage made new cars very expensive so people turned to used cars, and used car prices surged.

In 2021, interest rates were low and people were receiving stimulus cheques, so demand for used cars took off and dealerships that had previously cancelled subscriptions to CarGurus before the pandemic resubscribed. The company then purchased a majority stake in CarOffer in 2021, and bought the remainder of the company in August 2022. This acquisition has provided the company with a new avenue of growth, but has also caused their revenue to be extremely volatile since every transaction that takes place on CarOffer is recorded at gross value (i.e., a $25,000 vehicle sale would mean $25,000 in revenue, even if their actual gross margin on the deal only nets them $1000).

Today, the normalization of the auto market has exposed fundamental weaknesses in the company's business model. In the next 12-24 months, I am expecting things like intensifying competition from well-funded rivals to become more apparent, and an extremely low level of consumer confidence resulting from the tariffs to stall the auto industry to make growth much more difficult for them.

Right now, the stock is trading at what I consider to be an unjustified premium to industry peers. As such, I rate CarGurus a Sell.

Company Description

CarGurus is a digital automotive marketplace that connects buyers and sellers of new and used vehicles. They operate under a few different brands in different regions: CarGurus in the US, UK, and Canada, AutoList and CarOffer in the US, and PistonHeads in the UK. Their revenue primarily comes from subscriptions that car dealers pay to use their platform. They operate using a freemium model where dealers can list inventory for free, but then they have to pay to use more premium features and access leads. The company has just over 32 thousand paying dealers as of March 30, 2025. The remainder comes from platform advertising where car manufacturers (OEMs), insurance, and other companies in the car industry pay for ad placements.

The company has 2 segments:

  1. U.S. Marketplace: Derives revenue from marketplace services for customers within the U.S. The platform uses an algorithm called IMV (Instant Market Value) to assign a tag that assesses the deal (i.e., Great Deal, Good Deal, Fair Deal, High Priced, or Overpriced) which helps support their mission of being transparent.
  2. Digital Wholesale: Primarily derives revenue from Dealer-to-Dealer and Instant Max Cash Offer services sold on the CarOffer platform. You can think of CarOffer like a stock market for cars where prices are pre-determined based on factors like year, model, make, mileage, etc. and dealers can buy and sell user cars among themselves. The platform also streamlines the process by automating things like title transfers and payments. Instant Max Cash Offer allows car sellers to receive cash for their cars, and then connects the sale to a dealer in its network. It's essentially an additional way for dealers to get more inventory.

In terms of revenue, as of F2024 Year-End 89% of revenue came from dealer subscriptions and platform advertising, with the rest coming from the CarOffer services described above. However CarOffer is transaction based and much more cyclical, so revenue varies greatly year-to-year depending on how many transactions they process. This business model has a few pros and cons. For starters, the fact that most of their revenue is subscription based means their cash flow is more predictable and steady. Additionally, most of their sales are used cars, which are experiencing strong demand. However the digital wholesale segment is highly cyclical and now that consumer confidence is low, it will see a sharp decline. This revenue was already down significantly in Q1 2025.

Headwinds

Let's now discuss the problems we can see coming for CarGurus, besides the obvious ones stemming from tariffs and a potential recession, which is a rapidly changing situation that is very difficult to predict.

1. Competition/Increased Cost per Click

CarGurus was not the first online car marketplace (they were founded in 2006, while AutoTrader.com was founded in 1997) but they were an early mover in the industry, and their proprietary deal rating algorithm helps differentiate them. The business overall is very profitable and has return on invested capital that exceeds the industry norm:

The problem is that this attracts competition. CarGurus' business model proved how profitable this industry can be, so now other companies want a piece.

CarGurus has also relied on their high SEO ranking in Google search as a free source of traffic. However, the Google algorithm is changing and starting to prioritize paid search results more, and click-through rates for highly ranked SEO websites in general are declining. CarGurus' advantage has always been their network (lots of buyers and sellers), but this network's strength relies on customers visiting their platforms more than competing platforms, in addition to having dealers list their inventory. You need both for this to work, and the evidence suggests that getting users to visit the site will require more in ad spend going forward. And Google now offers their own services for selling used cars. They partnered with marketplaces like Dealer.com, AutoTrader, and Cars.com to pull vehicle inventory data into their listings. This allows dealers to showcase their vehicles directly on Google without relying on a third party platform like CarGurus. Many car buyers start their journey on Google, so this will lead to reduced traffic for them going forward. And their marketplace listings are connected to Google ads, so competitors can very easily used paid search to enhance the visibility of their listings. Since CarGurus has relied heavily on their SEO ranking, this presents a significant threat to them.

It's important for investors to understand that SEO is important for CarGurus. There's a reason why they disclose "Monthly Sessions" in their MD&A. It's a piece of evidence that shows how one side of their network is performing. And let's not forget, they ran into trouble with this in 2019 when the Google algorithm was changed, and their organic search volume declined while Cars.com increased significantly.

Although SEO is important, it's not everything for the company. Most of their search volume is actually direct. But there are other competitive threats to be aware of such as Cars.com, TrueCar, and AutoTrader. For example, Cars.com has evolved its platform from just a listing site into a technology company that can do things like marketing automation, a CRM tool, and they own AccuTrade which is a vehicle appraisal tool that competes with CarOffer. If they can keep improving their technology they could become more mission critical than CarGurus.

2. The Market Is Only So Big, and the Middle Class Is Struggling

This point requires a more long-term view of CarGurus. The used car market is highly cyclical and very mature, and CarGurus does need the used car market to be strong in order for more dealerships to exist to meet such demand, which results in more potential customers for them. The biggest customer group for used cars is the middle class given how expensive they have become, however the US economy is increasingly being split into haves and have-nots. The impact this will have on CarGurus will either be positive of negative. It can be positive if the surge in new car costs (a new car costs nearly $50k on average) leads to increased demand for used cars. For most US citizens, a car is a necessity and not a luxury, so demand will never go away. So a shrinking middle class may reduce overall affordability and put downward pressure on demand at the lower end of the used market, but it could also shift more buyers into the used car market from the new car market. However, if incomes continue to stagnate for middle income families, fewer people will be able to afford even used cars. Or, they will be forced to extend the lifespan of their existing vehicles, which is a trend that is already happening.

Furthermore, as of March 30, 2025, CarGurus has 25,153 paying dealers in the US, and 7,219 internationally. In the US, it's estimated that there are around 60,000 independent used car dealerships, and another 18,000 franchised car dealerships. This means CarGurus has already penetrated roughly one third of their total addressable market. And consider that some dealers already use the free version of their platform and don't feel the need to upgrade to a paid plan, and, of course, some go with the competitors like Cars.com and AutoTrader. CarGurus still hasn't seen their total number of paying dealers recover to what it was pre-pandemic:

To be clear, I am not making this point because I think CarGurus will reach a point where they stop growing. They can still expand internationally, and grow their ad business which has been a strong growth driver for them (even if it faces headwinds). But their current valuation (forward PE of 15.5x versus sector median of 12.7) suggests investors are expecting strong growth in earnings, which seems unlikely to me.

How Would a Recession Impact Them?

There are still questions as to how likely a recession is to occur in the US, and how severe it would be. What we know for now is that consumer spending is beginning to show signs of slowing, inflation remains somewhat elevated after a few very high years, and interest rates are still relatively high. All these factors could weigh on demand for big-ticket purchases like vehicles. Here's the impacts I'd expect we'd see for CarGurus:

  1. Slower Growth in Advertising Revenue as OEMs Cut Marketing Budgets: car dealers have historically cut marketing budgets very early in recessions. So we'd likely see slower growth, or even shrinkage, in their average revenue per subscribing dealer. Given how CarGurus stock is up nearly 40% over the past year despite these factors changing materially, it doesn't seem that this is priced in. This is also one of the company's fastest growing revenue streams, so this showing signs of slowing would likely make investors re-price the stock in their valuation models.
  2. Decreased consumer demand for cars: this one is more obvious, but in an economic slowdown where unemployment rates rise, there will be less demand for cars and CarGurus will have a harder time growing dealer count. Especially if inflation stays above 2% and interest rates don't go lower.
  3. Dealers postpone technology investments: Amid economic uncertainty, dealers that were previously interested in upgrade their tech capabilities and using CarGurus are going to hold off on those investments. CarGurus is primarily a lead generation/marketing tool for dealers, it is not mission critical. Although the company's business model is SaaS-like, it lacks the mission critical nature that makes other SaaS companies more resilient.

Conclusion

CarGurus created a very innovative business model that is undoubtedly highly profitable. However, I don't think the company's growth prospects justify their premium valuation of 15.5x forward earnings, versus the sector median of 12.7x. The business is also highly cyclical, and with consumer confidence as low as it is right now, we're likely to witness a cyclical bottom for at least the next 6 months.

I'm sure there will be some people that disagree with me, so feel free to lease your thoughts in the comments section.

data.title: XLY: Consumer Discretionary Dashboard For May
data.description:
This monthly article reports metrics in the consumer discretionary sector, aiming at a top-down analysis based on value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLY), Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (FDIS) and Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF (VCR), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance. A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

data.title: Luminar Q1 2025 Earnings: Austin Russell's Exit Shakes Outlook
data.description:
Luminar Technologies Overview

What was expected to be a routine quarterly earnings update with anticipation for an added dose of clarity on Luminar Technologies’ (NASDAQ:LAZR) revised operating plan has quickly turned into a corporate bombshell. In a surprise move, the company announced the abrupt departure of founder and CEO Austin Russell, following a Code of Business Conduct inquiry by the Board. Details remain scarce, and Russell’s sudden exit draws heightened scrutiny to Luminar’s fundamental outlook.

Recall that Russell, known for being a 2013 Thiel Fellow and one of the world’s youngest self-made billionaires, had long been a symbolic pillar of Luminar’s identity. His association with successes in the ecosystem of Peter Thiel’s prescient tech investments – an elite club that’s fostered trailblazers behind Palantir (PLTR), Figma, Scale.AI, and Ethereum (ETH-USD) – contributed significantly to investor enthusiasm in Luminar’s early years. With Russell now gone and incoming CEO Paul Ricci taking the reins, that symbolic halo has been stripped away, leaving investors with only the company’s fundamentals to judge – and the figures paint a sobering picture.

Despite some success in cutting costs, Luminar remains far from profitability – not because of insufficient scale, but because of an uncertain, and arguably incoherent, business roadmap. The highly anticipated update on its “new operating plan and go-forward strategy” depends heavily on the impending “Luminar Halo” program. But Halo isn’t entirely new news, with the revamped product introduced last year. And neither is it going to materialize in the near-term, given its lengthy go-to-market timeline that coincides with a cash runway projected only through the end of 2026. Coupled with broader industry volatility, tariff-driven macro headwinds, and rapidly evolving tech demands, the path forward looks increasingly precarious for Luminar.

With Russell’s departure removing the symbolic confidence once propping up investor sentiment, Luminar is now fully exposed to the realities of its fragile business fundamentals. The company’s path to profitability remains elusive, weighed down by strategic ambiguity, delayed commercialization timelines, and a liquidity runway that offers little margin for error. In the absence of near-term growth catalysts and amid mounting macro and industry-specific headwinds, Luminar’s outlook remains firmly skewed to the downside.

Lacking Traction in AV Theme

Autonomous mobility is regaining momentum as a major growth theme in 2025. This is largely propelled by the current U.S. administration’s deepening push for a more supportive regulatory framework, as well as a string of accelerating milestones across the industry.

Tesla’s (TSLA) recent “We, Robot” event in October 2024 was key to stoking investor excitement for the technology. This was helped by improved clarity on its plans to launch a fully autonomous Robotaxi and unsupervised FSD solution coming June. Leaders like Google’s Waymo (GOOG / GOOGL) are also rapidly expanding paid, unsupervised driverless rides to new U.S. cities including Austin and Atlanta, and even internationally to Tokyo. Meanwhile, Uber (UBER) has aggressively expanded its robotaxi ambitions as well through strategic partnerships with key players like WeRide (WRD), May Mobility, and Pony.ai (PONY).

These developments have renewed industry-wide traction across all approaches to autonomous mobility developments – whether camera-based, sensor-based, or hybrid systems. Yet despite having once been a frontrunner in LiDAR technology, Luminar has struggled to capture any real share of this revitalized interest. The company's inconsistent go-to-market strategy has ultimately blunted the impact of its early technical wins.

Most notably, Luminar failed to build on the brand awareness and commercial potential that came with high-profile integrations of its Iris sensor with global OEMs like Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF / OTCPK:MBGYY) and technology partners like Nvidia (NVDA). Mercedes was the first to bring Level 3 autonomy to market through its Drive Pilot system, while Nvidia has continued to scale its influence in ADAS and AV developments through its DRIVE Orin and Hyperion platforms. These partnerships were more than just technological wins – they were opportunities for Luminar to cement its brand and validate its competencies within a rapidly innovating field. Yet the company lacked follow-through in commercial execution and marketing amplification. This ultimately resulted in a structural failure by Luminar to convert its early technological lead into lasting commercial momentum – an essential step for scaling revenue and unlocking the operating leverage needed to achieve profitability.

Now, the company has shifted its focus to the new Luminar Halo program – a standardized, potentially more scalable approach intended to improve time-to-market and streamline deployment for OEMs. But the AV race has moved beyond R&D, with OEMs under growing pressure to ramp up deployment to stay competitive in the fast-converging world of connected and intelligent mobility. In this environment, Luminar’s reset strategy may be “too little, too late”, increasing struggles to gain relevance, let alone leadership.

Limited Fundamental Catalysts in Sight

With visionary founder Russell no longer at the helm, investor scrutiny is expected to shift more sharply toward Luminar’s fundamentals – especially as its near-term growth story weakens. Although the new Halo program aims to improve scalability for both the company and its OEM partners, its extended timeline to commercialization sets Luminar further behind in the AV race. The fact that Halo’s go-to-market is expected to coincide with Luminar’s tightening liquidity runway is another critical challenge, as it heightens the company’s exposure to execution risks.

During the company’s Q1 earnings update, management disclosed total liquidity of approximately $400 million. This includes $138 million in cash and marketable securities, a $50 million undrawn credit facility, and about $200 million that can be accessed through its at-the-market equity financing program. This funding roadmap is expected to support operations through at least the end of 2026. However, this projection likely already assumes the full benefit of recently implemented cost actions, which have driven material annualized savings:

Specifically, versus a year ago, if you look at our non-GAAP OpEx, a good proxy to measure the cash savings from our restructuring actions, those have declined by about $115 million on an annualized basis.

Our quarterly stock-based compensation, a good proxy for the stock savings, has declined by almost $100 million on an annualized basis. Overall, I'm proud that we're executing on what we set out to do in reducing costs in our business and extending our financial runway.

Source: Luminar Technologies 1Q25 Earnings Call Transcript

These efforts were also reflected in Luminar’s revised FY25 operating expense outlook, which is now down from a previously guided average in the high $30 million range per quarter to the low $30 million range.

Yet the timing doesn’t align with the company’s commercial prospects. Halo sales aren’t expected to contribute meaningfully to Luminar’s P&L until at least 2027, given the product isn’t expected to launch until late 2026 at the earliest. Meanwhile, the company remains in what management calls the “middle innings” of a capital-intensive investment cycle for the Halo program. Compounding this is the persistence of legacy Iris program wind-down costs through year-end, leaving little room for positive surprises on the financial front.

Limited accretive growth drivers between now and the Halo program’s ramp-up punts unfavourably against Luminar’s diminishing liquidity buffer. It risks significantly increasing Luminar’s exposure to execution missteps and unexpected macro and/or company-specific headwinds. While management sees their 10% to 20% revenue growth guidance for FY25 as “conservative”, citing a “50% haircut” to industry forecasts, the company’s already experiencing erosion to that buffer due to the “volatile geopolitical and macro environment”. This is evident in the $1 million tariff-related headwind that Luminar had experienced in the first quarter. Although management expects the impact to be manageable through real-time price negotiations with customers, it remains a dynamic and uncertain situation – which is an unwelcomed risk overhang for the stock.

Luminar’s customer concentration further compounds the fundamental risk. The company is heavily reliant on its partnership with Volvo (OTCPK:VOLAF / OTCPK:VLVLY / OTCPK:VOLVF). This is corroborated by management’s attribution of Luminar’s recent cost improvements to the successful launch of its “first global production vehicle with the Volvo EX90” last year. But here’s the issue – Volvo has roughly 15% revenue exposure to China, which is an increasingly sensitive market amid the ongoing U.S.-China tariff standoff. Despite recent signs of de-escalation, meaningful resolution of these trade tensions remains a wild card. And any ensuing tariff shocks could further pressure Luminar’s already fragile cost structure.

Analysts have also flagged growing risks tied to Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY / OTCPK:NSANF), which is Luminar’s other key customer that’s now facing deteriorating demand:

Winnie Dong

I think Nissan, you may have heard, through the media that's going through some of this own challenges…I'm just curious to hear if this impacts your conversation with them, if at all, in terms of their conversion to Halo future business opportunities.

Tom Fennimore

No. The short answer is no. I would say everybody in the automotive industry is kind of going through what Nissan is going through, what Luminar is going through, right? It's kind of tough times with the current macroeconomic environment as well as the geopolitical uncertainty. The good thing about a company like Nissan is they're committed to new technologies and making their vehicles safer. And that commitment is at the peak of the cycle and at the trough of the cycle. So we haven't seen any change there in Nissan's development efforts around Halo.

Source: Luminar Technologies 1Q25 Earnings Call Transcript

While Luminar has sought to downplay these concerns by pointing to the mission-critical role of its technologies in vehicle safety, the reality is that its growth trajectory is closely tethered to customer unit volumes. A weakening sales outlook at Nissan, therefore, inevitably introduces heightened execution and demand risks for Luminar – something that remains underappreciated by management.

Taken together, these factors suggest Luminar is entering a critical stretch with limited margin for error. With liquidity steadily depleting and commercial catalysts still on the distant horizon, the company’s fundamental risk profile continues to skew decisively to the downside.

Final Thoughts

Luminar enters a pivotal chapter – one marked by executive transition, a delayed product roadmap, and thinning liquidity that raises the stakes for execution on a limited margin of error. The departure of visionary founder Russell has stripped away a key symbolic anchor, shifting investor focus squarely onto the company’s shaky fundamentals. With meaningful contributions from the Luminar Halo program not expected until late 2026 or early 2027, and cash burn continuing in the interim, downside risks remain elevated. Further compounding this pressure is the company's recent delay in filing its 10-Q, which raises delisting risks due to non-compliance with Nasdaq filing rules. This could dampen near-term appetite for the stock, particularly from institutional investors with stringent mandates, which often play a pivotal role in sustaining valuation support and trading liquidity.

Still, there are potential offsetting tailwinds that could preclude a full-on materialization of the downside scenario. The appointment of corporate veteran Paul Ricci as CEO brings a highly welcomed infusion of business-focused leadership, which could help address Luminar’s long-standing commercial shortcomings as discussed in earlier sections. The stock’s depressed valuation, when weighed against its proven technical IP, also makes it a viable takeover candidate – especially for larger technology players and auto OEMs looking to shore up their AV stack. Additionally, with a short float north of 20%, any positive catalyst could spark a short squeeze, temporarily lifting sentiment in the stock.

But none of the aforementioned tailwinds are expected to sustainably reverse the broader narrative: Luminar’s long-term prospects hinge on execution, which remains an overhanging uncertainty in the underlying business. Until Luminar’s new strategy translates into tangible, accretive results, investor confidence will likely remain fragile, leaving the stock’s path to recovery uncertain.

data.title: TIM S.A. Q1: Good Results And Momentum Are Interesting, But Not Enough
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am raising my recommendation from sell to hold on TIM S.A. (NYSE:TIMB) shares. This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published on May 14 here on Seeking Alpha.

In this article, I will revisit my thesis and highlight where I was wrong. Since the publication of the article with the recommendation to sell, the total return has been 16.11%. But as Charlie Munger would say, it is important to revisit mistakes to learn from them.

Additionally, I will now analyze the Q1 results published on May 5. I will also explain the valuation and the reason for raising the recommendation from sell to hold and not buy.

TIM

TIM is one of the giants of the Brazilian telecommunications sector and has been operating in the country for 27 years. Its main competitors are Vivo (NYSE:VIV), Claro (a subsidiary of América Móvil), and recently Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU).

The company has more than 60 million mobile customers and is the leader in 4G and 5G coverage in Brazil. The company's main shareholder is Telecom Italia (OTCPK:TIIAY) with 67% of the shares.

Revisiting the Thesis

In my initial coverage article, I recommended selling due to increased competition in the sector. It is important to remember that the Brazilian population has high household debt, which in my view reduces growth.

In addition, existing players were already dealing with competition among themselves, however, the entry of Nu Holdings into this market raised concerns about intensifying competition.

It is important to remember that Nu has 100 million Brazilian customers, and its mobile phone service directly impacts TIM, which has high exposure to the mobile segment.

As we can see above, the stock has risen significantly since then. What has happened since then is that TIM's operating income has improved, and competition has not yet increased significantly.

Now, I will analyze the results that the company released on May 5th and link them with the new vision of the thesis. The company exceeded the market's net income expectations.

Net Revenue

TIM's net revenue changed +4.9% y/y and -3.6% q/q to BRL 6.39 billion ($1.12 billion). This performance was driven by the 6.2% increase in mobile services, especially in postpaid. The quarterly reduction, however, is due to seasonality.

As for perspectives, I believe that TIM will continue to reduce churn, migrate customers to more profitable plans and perform upselling. However, I remain curious about Nu Holdings' entry into this market and the impact that this will have in the future.

Given Nu's 100 million customers in Brazil, a 15% conversion of its customers to mobile phone service will represent a significant loss of market share for TIM and its competitors.

Costs, Expenses and Margins

TIM managed to control costs and expenses on an annual basis. The company's EBITDA margin changed by 0.80% y/y and -2.2% q/q. The annual increase reflects the control of operating costs, while the quarterly decrease reflects a one-off pressure from roaming and content expenses.

It is vital that the company remains efficient and closely controls costs. The industry scenario is complex, as there is little room for growth in new users, and existing users face strong competition from other players.

Debt and CapEx

The Net Debt Total/Normalized EBITDA metric reached 0.86x, we can infer that the company's debt remains healthy.

Capex fell 1.1% y/y and 2.6% q/q to BRL 1.33bn ($232m). Currently, CapEx represents 20.9% of the company's net revenue.

Investors who follow TIM's thesis should pay attention to the company's capital discipline. The business model requires significant investments in fixed assets, especially commitments to 5G. Therefore, it is extremely important to analyze in all results whether the company demonstrates efficiency in capital allocation.

Net Income

The company's normalized net income increased by +56% y/y and -23.2% q/q to BRL 810 million ($142 million). The annual expansion was driven by expense control and improved operating income.

I assess the 1Q25 results as interesting, given that the company managed to improve revenues, costs, and expenses, and its results were driven entirely by efficiency. Before we change our recommendation, let's talk about valuation.

Valuation

To value the company, we will use the P/E multiple. In this sector, I see many investors using EV/EBITDA as their valuation, but this is wrong, as these companies have inflated depreciation and amortization due to the nature of their activity. This gives a false impression of high EBITDA and leaves the multiple artificially low.

As we can see, the tool indicates a favorable valuation. However, I see that the best way to evaluate the company is through the P/E FWD against the sector itself. We have the sector trading at 12.67x earnings versus the company trading at 11.51x earnings, and then we have an upside of 10%.

These results and the 10% upside are enough for me to upgrade the recommendation from sell to hold. However, I still see many challenges regarding the competition, little growth potential, and an upside not large enough to justify a buy recommendation.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

I see two major risks to the thesis, remembering that the risk is that the investor will not buy the stock and miss out on new highs. The first risk is related to the company's profile.

Brazil is currently experiencing a complex macroeconomic environment and extremely high interest rates. TIM operates in a defensive sector and may attract investor buying flow in a more hostile economic environment.

As we have seen, the company has high operating margins and healthy debt. If TIM maintains disciplined capital allocation, it could increase the dividends distributed, which could also attract investors seeking dividends.

Last but not least, Seeking Alpha’s powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy on the stock. This goes against my skeptical recommendation to hold.

The Bottom Line

TIM reported good results in Q1. Despite the competition, the company increased net revenue by 4.9% y/y and had an 80 bps y/y increase in EBITDA margin, showing rigidity in cost and expense control.

However, I have seen investors using the wrong method to evaluate the stock. The EV/EBITDA indicator can give a wrong view, and the best option is to analyze it using P/E, which provides a relatively small upside of 10%.

Based on this analysis, I am raising my recommendation to sell to hold the stock. In my view, the effects of the competitive environment have not yet been reflected in the results, and the upside is too small to take the risk.

data.title: Hafnia Limited: Why It's Still A Great Option
data.description:
On January 12th of this year, I wrote a report on Hafnia Limited (NYSE:HAFN) in which I provided an initial overview of the company and the strengths I identified relative to the current state of the industry. In that report, titled "Hafnia Limited: Not Just a Dividend Paying Machine", I explored the current structure of fossil fuel transportation globally and how the company is positioned in this particular scenario.

In this report, I intend to provide an update on the investment thesis developed during early 2025, as well as a brief summary of the company's new results published for 4Q2024. In this regard, I intend to focus on the development of the conflict in the Red Sea and the Suez Canal, the new regulations from the Office of Foreign Asset Control (OFAC), and how these could potentially affect Hafnia and, ultimately, the current state of the market in general.

In my opinion, and despite the ups and downs on the international scene following Trump's arrival at the White House, Hafnia remains a highly attractive option. The company continues to deliver high shareholder returns through dividend payments and share buybacks. At the same time, it demonstrates solid management with a capital structure in the process of deleveraging.

Update

I would like to begin updating the investment thesis with one of the points I highlighted in the previous report, which was the relationship between transported tons and international freight prices for different types of ships. As we mentioned at the time, it was clear that over time, there was a correlation between the number of tons transported and freight rates per vessel.

In January 2025, there was still a spread between the two, something we said would be corrected. Looking at Hafnia's latest report, we see that for LR1 vessels, this gap narrowed significantly. It can still be seen in other segments, but we estimate that this will also tend to normalize over the course of the year. During 4Q2024, the cause of the drop in transported tonnage can be traced to the closure of the Suez Canal in the Red Sea during this period. The drop in transported cargo led to a drop in freight prices, but as the situation normalized, so did prices.

Red Sea Scenario

This brings us to the topic of the closure of the Suez Canal as a result of the Red Sea crisis. In this regard, the previous report mentioned something about it, but I think it's important to delve deeper into the conflict, given the consequences it had for the company and the circumstantial changes in the international landscape.

Focusing solely on the fossil fuel transport market, it can be seen that with the closure of the Suez Canal, trade flow began a downward trend when evaluating the number of barrels transported per day. The question of why this maritime passage is important is entirely valid at this point, and I would like to briefly review it to contextualize the situation. The Suez Canal emerged as an alternative to crossing the waterways between Europe and the Middle East, so its importance for the fossil fuel industry can be seen. Even so, the canal is an alternative to shipping around the Cape of Good Hope for two fundamental reasons. First, because it allows the passage of larger vessels and because its infrastructure supports a much higher shipping flow. Second, because it shortens travel times, allowing for lower costs.

The closure of the Suez Canal due to a series of conflicts with a group of Houthi pirates in the Red Sea forced shipping companies to re-arrange cargo and resume routes that cross the Cape of Good Hope to Southern Africa. This generated a sharp increase in shipping prices, which was not fully translated into increased profitability, given the proportional increase in costs. On the other hand, in the case of the oil and gas industry, it led to a significant decrease in the number of barrels transported per day.

Now, it is important to determine what impact a resolution of the conflict and the reopening of transport through the Suez Canal could have on Hafnia. Initially, as can be seen, profits would be lower, but an increase in transported volume is also expected. All of this would generate a slightly negative, but practically neutral, impact. Evaluating this scenario becomes important following Trump's arrival at the White House. The American president seeks to end international conflicts, and the problems in the Red Sea are no exception. In fact, the Houthis are already intending to end the conflict, according to reports from Washington.

At this point, the loss the company would appear to incur in the short term if the conflict is resolved doesn't seem significant. This is especially true because in the medium term, once international trade is regularized, it's likely that even though charter rates will decrease, so will transportation costs. At the same time, I expect an increase in volume and less wear and tear on vessels, which in the long run will mean financial savings for companies like Hafnia.

OFAC Listing of Vessels & Sanctions

Another key point to consider was the sanctions imposed by the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) on a series of vessels linked to trade with Iran. The decision was made due to suspicions that the vessels were transporting Iranian oil, a country sanctioned by the United States and banned from trade with a large number of nations. The practices allegedly involved the transfer of oil from one vessel to another in areas near Iraq, Iran, the UAE, and the Gulf of Oman.

According to the company, this OFAC vessel listing imposes a mismatch between global supply and demand, as it entails sanctioning a fleet equivalent to the entire list of new vessels scheduled for 2025. It is important to note that the sanctioned vessels are an average of 17 years old, which could imply a de facto conversion of these units to scrap. While this has clear impacts on older vessels, it also affects vessels with new technology, since, rather than complementing older vessels, they must fulfill the role of replacing technology more quickly than the market expected.

On the other hand, the company estimates that there are a potential 410 vessels around the world that will have traded with Russia by 2024 and are therefore susceptible to being added to the sanctions list. This scenario would add even more pressure to this market. On the other hand, both China and India are expected to discontinue trade relations with vessels involved in hydrocarbon imports. Hafnia estimates that this decision would involve replacing the equivalent of 100 Suezmax vessels to meet the requirements of both countries.

This reinforces one of the theories we mentioned in the previous report: the fact that vessels are becoming obsolete faster than the industry can manufacture new ones. This reality could lead to a decrease in the number of tons available for transport, which would raise prices if we take into account that international charter prices can be viewed as equivalent to several commodities.

Demand & Supply Update

The last point I'd like to highlight is an update on the current global supply and demand context in relation to the fossil fuel transportation market. What can be seen is that in the first half of the year, refineries are maintaining higher production than in the same period in 2022, 2024, and 2025. On the other hand, what can also be seen is that vessel utilization is at average levels of recent years. This, as Hafnia also points out, could boost demand.

In this regard, Søren Winther, the company's Vice President of Sales, stated the following in the last conference call:

Moving on to Slide 14. Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook for the product tanker segment into 2025. We continue to observe an increasing average age of the global product tanker fleet, and this trend has significant implications as older vessels are underutilized due to customer preference for younger tonnage. Global oil demand also remains robust. For the full year 2024, global oil demand has increased by 0.87 million barrels per day with a further increase of 1.1 million barrels per day to be expected for 2025.

Additionally, refinery maintenance accumulated at the start of the year are expected to be lower in the next months, paving the way for higher refinery runs. This will likely result in increased production and export of refined products, boosting global demand for product tankers.

4Q2024 Results

With the investment thesis update complete, I'd like to continue with the 4Q2024 results. While it's true that margins contracted sharply last quarter, both the annualized ROE and ROIC show values ​​that, in my view, are very solid. In the former case, the annualized ROE reached 34.5% while the ROIC was 25%.

If we analyze the metrics for 4Q2024, we see that the company's net profit was $79.6 million, compared to $176.4 million in 4Q2023, which implies a decline that cannot be ignored. However, I would like to focus on the company's annual metrics and compare them to 2023. In 2024, Hafnia achieved a Net Profit of $774 million, compared to $793.3 million in 2023. This equates to a profit per share of $1.52.

Looking at Adjusted EBITDA, we see a similar trend. Fourth quarter 2024 closed at $131.2 million, compared to $234.5 million in fourth quarter 2023. However, when considering the full year, Adjusted EBITDA for 2024 was $992.3 million, compared to $1,012.9 million in 2023. At current values, the EV/EBITDA ratio shows an impressive value of 3.6x, something that, in my opinion, is extremely attractive if we add up all the potential described above.

One of the positive aspects I had highlighted that reversed slightly this quarter was the evolution of net debt, on the one hand, and the value of Hafnia's fleet, on the other. One of the things I had previously highlighted was the company's deleveraging due to an increase in cash, which had led to a significant reduction in net debt. This was even more surprising considering that this had occurred in a context of increasing fleet value. Both processes were slightly eroded in this last quarter. I think this is not a cause for concern at the moment, but it is worth monitoring how it evolves in the future.

Risks

Before concluding this report, I would like to briefly mention the potential risks the company may face in the future. First, and in line with the latter, the decline in the fleet's value could be a factor to consider. If OFAC continues to take action and the sanctioned vessels are treated as scrap due to their age, this would jeopardize the value of similar vessels held by companies like Hafnia.

Secondly, if international oil prices continue to decline, we could enter a reversal of the trend. Given this change in international crude oil prices, refineries could have less incentive to produce, leading to a reduction in demand for vessels transporting processed fuels.

Finally, although there has been news in recent days that appears to suggest the United States will halt tariff increases on imports, the issue is still ongoing. Should these barriers to international trade be reactivated, it will undoubtedly have a negative impact on global markets, and the shipping industry will be no exception.

Conclusion

Despite the caveats I mentioned previously, I believe the outlook described in the first part, which delves into the thesis detailed in the previous report, is more than interesting. I view Hafnia positively for the medium term, and I believe the combination of good management, an appropriate margin of safety, a solvent financial position, a favorable international environment, and the strong dividend payouts I mentioned in the previous report continues to make Hafnia an excellent option to hold in your portfolio. For this reason, I believe the company continues to represent a strong buy.

data.title: 3 Great REITs For Buy-And-Hold Investors
data.description:
REITs have done poorly in recent years, and this is causing investors to forget just how exceptionally rewarding they have been over the long run.

Taking the longest possible time frame, which is over 50 years, REITs have easily outperformed most other asset classes, including the S&P 500 (SPY), Tech stocks (QQQ), bonds, and gold (GLD):

Moreover, these averages include the good, the bad, and the ugly.

Some specific REIT business models were a lot more rewarding than the average, earning closer to 15-20% average annual returns.

Today, even those highly lucrative REITs are priced at historically low valuations, and in what follows, I will highlight three such REITs to buy and hold "forever".

Their business models are like magic. They create far more shareholder value than others, resulting in persistent outperformance.

CubeSmart (CUBE)

Over the long run, the most rewarding property sector has been self-storage.

Those REITs have earned near-20% average annual returns for the 28-year period ending in 2021:

They have been so incredibly rewarding because of three key reasons:

1) The large scale of REITs was a major advantage in this particular property sector as it allowed them to develop valuable brands (think "Public Storage"), better marketing strategies with national advertising campaigns, and superior pricing models.

2) They were able to develop a lot of new properties at high initial yields as demand was growing, resulting in large spreads over their cost of capital.

3) They were also able to acquire properties from smaller, less sophisticated private landlords, and then change brands and improve operations to rapidly create a lot of value.

And CubeSmart has done even better than the average of its peer group.

It has managed to consistently grow faster and earn better returns:

We think that CubeSmart stands out for three key reasons, which we expect to lead to more outperformance over the long run:

1) Its properties are located in the most dense markets, where people live in small homes and real estate is expensive. Brooklyn would be a good example. As a result, these properties enjoy strong and growing demand, but their supply is strictly limited:

2) CubeSmart also operates a capital-light management business, providing its scale, brand, and expertise to other smaller self-storage property owners. It's win-win because CubeSmart is often able to boost profits for the landlord, increase their property value, turn it into a passive business, and they then earn fees for it.

3) Finally, CubeSmart is the right size. It is big enough to enjoy economies of scale, but not too big to slow down its growth. Its market cap is $9 billion, but it is still much smaller than its close peers like Public Storage (PSA) and Extra Space Storage (EXR). The smaller size should allow it to grow faster over the long run.

Despite all of that, CubeSmart has tanked with the rest of the REIT market in this 3-year-long bear market, and now trades at a historically low valuation:

Its price to FFO is just 16x, which is a discount relative to its lower quality peers:

FFO Multiple
CubeSmart 16x
Public Storage 18x
Extra Space Storage 18x

We think that this is an opportunity for long-term oriented investors to build a position in a high-quality REIT that should outperform the broader market with less risk over the long run.

The ~5% dividend yield, coupled with its 6-9% average annual long-term growth, should be enough to deliver double-digit total returns, and the REIT could enjoy another 20% upside as it reprices at closer to 20x FFO, which has historically been the norm for this REIT.

Lastly, the REIT has a strong BBB investment grade rating and is not heavily impacted by the surge in interest rates.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR)

The second-best performing property sector over the long run has been industrial.

It has earned a near 16% average annual total return:

The property sector did so well because it benefited from the rapid growth of e-commerce.

This trend continues today and could even accelerate with the AI revolution, as it will allow e-commerce companies to improve their marketing, cut down on costs, offer even lower prices, and faster and cheaper shipping.

The AI revolution is also making it way easier to start new e-commerce businesses, which is expected to lead to a boom in new business formation, all of which will need some industrial space.

At the same time, industrial landlords also benefit from the growing trend of onshoring, which will only accelerate with AI and robotics, as it will close the gap in manufacturing costs. This is even more true with the tariffs being imposed.

So the long-term demand for industrial space looks bright. Yet, these REITs are today discounted because the sector is temporarily oversupplied:

The good news is that the new construction activity has dropped to a historically low level as a result of the high interest rates and construction costs.

This should lead to a strong acceleration in rent growth in the coming years and remove the overhang that has hurt the market sentiment of these REITs.

And Rexford Industrial stands out in this property sector.

It has done even better than the average of industrial REITs over the long run:

Its outperformance is the result of its focus on Southern California, which is commonly perceived to be the strongest industrial market in the world.

It is strategically located right by the Pacific and the Mexican border. Therefore, it is the entry and exit to the United States for importers and exporters. California is also a major consumer economy.

It is resulting in growing demand for space, but the new supply is highly constrained because most land is already built out:

Therefore, I think that REXR could be one of the biggest winners in the AI revolution. It owns highly desirable properties that will grow in demand and enjoy significant barriers to entry.

Yet, the REIT is today heavily discounted, trading at its lowest valuation in a long time:

This chart may lead you to think that the company is facing severe headwinds, but in reality, its cash flows and dividends are hitting new all-time highs. The company just released its Q1 results, and it grew its FFO per share by another 6.9%.

Yes, its growth has slowed down due to the oversupply, but since this is just temporary, it is resulting in a great opportunity for long-term oriented investors.

The stock now trades at just 15x FFO, down from over 40x at its peak.

You earn a 5% dividend yield that's well-covered, and the REIT is expected to grow at around 7-8% per year over the long run, resulting in double-digit total returns.

Historically, the REIT has also commonly traded at 20-25x FFO, meaning that it has at least 25% upside to its fair value.

VICI Properties (VICI)

Talking about properties that enjoy significant barriers-to-entry, it is hard to beat VICI Properties.

It is the leading casino net lease REIT, and it owns properties like the Caesars Palace, the Venetian, and the MGM Grand on the Las Vegas strip:

These are truly one-of-a-kind properties. The Las Vegas strip is already built out, and these properties require licenses that are very difficult to obtain.

Over the long run, this unique focus on high-quality casinos has resulted in significant market outperformance:

But today, the stock is relatively cheap, likely because the market seems to fear that we could be headed into a recession.

However, what it is overlooking is that VICI is a triple net lease REIT, meaning that its properties are under very long leases that result in highly consistent and predictable cash flow.

Its average remaining lease term is over 40 years. The leases include annual rent escalations and inflation protection. Tenants are even responsible for all property expenses, including maintenance.

As a result, its cash flow was resilient even to the pandemic, which was the worst possible crisis for a casino operator.

Its tenants are highly dependent on these properties, and they keep paying their rent in full and on time because they have no other option but to keep these assets under their control.

VICI is offered today at 12x FFO. It pays a 5.5% dividend yield, and it should be able to grow by about 5% per year over the long run.

The yield plus the growth should again result in double-digit total returns, and there is potential for a bit of upside from multiple expansion on top of that. This should be enough for VICI to keep outperforming the market, and that is why it is a core holding in our portfolio at High Yield Landlord.
